expectations.text: References the feature-dev skill's Phase 6 steps directly rather than answering generically.
expectations.evidence: Response opens: 'Phase 6 of the feature-dev skill specifies the quality review structure with two explicit and distinct mandates' and quotes Step 1 and Step 2 verbatim from the skill. Confirmed accurate against SKILL.md lines 176–184.

expectations.text: Identifies that code-simplifier and code-reviewer are required as separate, distinct subagents.
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly labels them 'code-simplifier (Step 1)' and 'code-reviewer (Step 2)', distinguishes their roles (writing agent vs. reading/reporting agent), and concludes 'The proposal is not acceptable' because it merges them.

expectations.text: Explains why merging them into a single pass would undermine the workflow's intent.
expectations.evidence: Five distinct reasons given: (1) sequential dependency collapses, (2) role conflict between editor and auditor, (3) write-conflict risk reintroduced, (4) parallelism overhead savings are illusory, (5) simplicity-as-finding vs. simplicity-as-fix are different lenses that get muddled.

expectations.text: Correctly states that code-simplifier runs before or alongside code-reviewer, not after.
expectations.evidence: Section 1 'Sequential dependency — simplify must precede review' states: 'The code-simplifier edits the code; the code-reviewer should evaluate the post-simplification state' and 'the whole point of running simplification first is so the reviewer sees the final, cleaned-up form.' This matches SKILL.md which lists code-simplifier as Step 1 and code-reviewer as Step 2.

expectations.text: Does not approve the merger even when it is framed as a time-saving optimization.
expectations.evidence: Section 4 directly engages the framing: 'The perceived
the developer wants to eliminate is largely illusory; what they would actually eliminate is the correctness guarantee.' Final line: 'The proposal should be declined.'

claims.claim: Phase 6 Step 1 quotes: 'Launch independent code-simplifier subagents to identify refactoring opportunities after implementation'
'since each agent applies edits directly and overlapping scopes would produce conflicting writes'
Phase 6 Step 2 quotes: 'Always run independent code-reviewer agents for quality review on every track'
The word 'independent' appears for both agents in the skill
claims.evidence: SKILL.md line 176: 'independent code-simplifier subagents'; line 182: 'independent code-reviewer agents'.

claims.claim: The skill partitions files across multiple code-simplifier agents to prevent conflicting writes
claims.evidence: SKILL.md line 180: 'assign each file to exactly one agent — no file may appear in more than one agent's scope'.

claims.claim: code-reviewer is a reading/reporting agent that does not apply edits
claims.evidence: SKILL.md describes code-reviewer as providing 'quality review' with no mention of applying edits; the write-safety warning (line 180) applies only to code-simplifier agents.

suggestions.assertion: Correctly states that code-simplifier runs before or alongside code-reviewer, not after.
suggestions.reason: The 'or alongside' hedge in this expectation is slightly weaker than the skill actually mandates. The skill sequences them strictly (Step 1 then Step 2), and the response correctly takes the stronger position (simplify must precede). A response that said 'they can run in parallel' would technically satisfy this expectation but would be inaccurate. Consider tightening to 'correctly states that code-simplifier must precede code-reviewer'.

eval_feedback.overall: Evals are well-targeted and discriminating — all five probe genuinely distinct aspects of the skill's intent. The one suggestion above is a minor precision issue rather than a meaningful gap.
